name: Warm Springs
short_description: BLM public land near Riverton offering free rockhounding for agate and jasper specimens. Flat terrain at 6,648 ft elevation makes this an accessible site for collectors of all skill levels.
description:
Warm Springs is a public BLM rockhounding site in Fremont County, Wyoming, offering free access to collectors seeking agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. Located at moderate elevation in the Wind River Basin area, this site features relatively flat terrain that makes it approachable for both beginners and experienced rockhounders.

The geology here reflects the Basin and Range province characteristic of central Wyoming, where Tertiary volcanic activity and subsequent erosional processes have concentrated silica-rich minerals in surface deposits and wash areas. Agate and jasper nodules formed within ancient volcanic and sedimentary layers, making them accessible through surface collection and careful excavation of weathered material.

The landscape is typical of high-desert Wyoming - sagebrush scrub with scattered outcrops and dry washes that concentrate heavier specimens. The relatively flat slopes and open terrain make it easier to survey large areas compared to more mountainous rockhounding sites in the state.

Collectors should expect to find both polished-quality agates and colorful jasper specimens, though material varies by specific collecting area within the site. The proximity to Riverton (38 miles) provides essential services, though you should arrive fully prepared with water and supplies.

As a BLM site, Warm Springs operates under recreational use guidelines that limit daily collection to 25 pounds per person and prohibit commercial operations. This ensures sustainable use while allowing continued public access for rockhound enthusiasts.
history: This region lies within the Wind River Basin, a major structural depression formed during Laramide orogeny. Miocene-age volcanism and subsequent weathering of pyroclastic deposits created ideal conditions for agate and jasper nodule formation, with silica-rich groundwater precipitating chalcedony minerals in fractures and porous zones. The modern collecting sites preserve these specimens as surface-accessible material following millions of years of erosion and alluvial transport.
tips: Focus your search along dry washes and eroded hillsides where water action has exposed and concentrated heavier mineral specimens over time. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and digging tools (small pry bar, chisel, trowel) to extract specimens from weathered volcanic matrix - many pieces are partially exposed in sandy material. Look for translucent to opaque nodules ranging from marble to fist size, with banding patterns typical of agate and the solid red, orange, or multicolored hues of jasper. Early morning collecting provides the best visibility and cooler temperatures during the warmer months.
terrain_notes: Elevation 6,648 feet with flat to gently rolling terrain composed of sagebrush desert scrub. Soil is sandy to gravelly with exposed rocky outcrops and dry wash areas. Walking distance from parking is typically 0.5 to 2 miles depending on which section you access. Footing is generally stable on hardpan and sandy surfaces, though some loose rock and uneven ground exists near washes. Vegetation is sparse, providing minimal shade.
best_season: May - September. Higher elevation (6,648 ft) keeps temperatures moderate during summer, while late spring through early fall offers dry conditions ideal for rockhounding. Winter snow and spring mud make access difficult; early summer heat (80-85°F) is manageable with proper hydration.
written_directions: From Riverton, take WY-135 north approximately 25 miles toward Shoshoni. Turn west on a BLM access road (specific road designation should be verified with local BLM office). Continue approximately 8-10 miles on graded dirt road to reach main collecting areas near the Warm Springs drainage. GPS coordinates 42.490826, -108.172096 mark the primary site. High-clearance or 4WD vehicle recommended, especially after rain.
road_conditions: Graded dirt BLM road, generally passable for high-clearance vehicles. May become rough or muddy after precipitation. Check with Riverton BLM office for current road status before collecting.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas exist along BLM roads near collecting zones. No developed parking lot; plan to park along road shoulders or designated BLM turnarounds.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect from surface only or by hand tools. Do not collect from private property adjacent to BLM land. Pack out all trash. No collection of petrified wood, fossils, or protected specimens.
rules:
Personal use collection only - no commercial sales
Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA regulations
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Hand tools only (pry bars, chisels, small picks acceptable)
No collection from private land
Pack out all trash
Spread your collecting to avoid surface disturbance
nearest_services: Riverton, 38 miles south. Gas, food, water, and supplies available. No services at the site.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers. Unimproved dirt road to parking area requires high-clearance or 4WD vehicle. Once parked, walking distances of 0.5 to 2 miles across sagebrush desert on sandy and gravelly soil. Loose rock and uneven ground near washes present minor hazards. No wheelchair access. Sturdy footwear essential. Minimal shade and full sun exposure.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Warm Springs, Wyoming?
faq.answer: Warm Springs is known for agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens. Agates typically display banding patterns in whites, grays, and earth tones, while jasper occurs in reds, oranges, and multicolors. Most specimens range from marble to fist size and are found as surface-accessible nodules or weathered pieces in sandy matrix.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Warm Springs free, or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Warm Springs is on BLM public land and completely free to rockhound. No permit is required for recreational personal use collection. However, you must follow BLM regulations: 25 pounds per day maximum, personal use only, and hand tools only. Commercial collection is prohibited.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Riverton at Warm Springs?
faq.answer: May through September offers the best rockhounding conditions. At 6,648 feet elevation, summer temperatures remain moderate (80-85°F) compared to lower Wyoming sites. Late spring and early fall have ideal dry conditions. Winter and early spring bring snow and mud that make BLM road access difficult or impossible.

faq.question: Is Warm Springs rockhounding site suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Warm Springs is excellent for beginners and families. The flat to gently rolling terrain is easy to navigate, specimens are surface-accessible, and no special skills or equipment are needed. Children ages 6 and up can participate easily. Just bring sun protection, plenty of water, and hand tools like small pry bars or chisels.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring for rockhounding at Warm Springs, Wyoming?
faq.answer: Bring a 5-gallon bucket, hand tools (pry bar, chisel, trowel, small pick), work gloves, and sturdy boots. Pack 2-3 gallons of water per person, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and a field guidebook for specimen identification. A GPS unit or smartphone is helpful given the remote location. No motorized excavation equipment is allowed.

faq.question: How far is Warm Springs rockhounding site from town, and what road conditions should I expect?
faq.answer: Warm Springs is 38 miles from Riverton, accessible via WY-135 north and BLM access roads. The final 8-10 miles are on graded dirt BLM roads requiring a high-clearance or 4WD vehicle, especially after rain. Cell service is spotty. Verify current road conditions with the Riverton BLM office before driving out, as remote roads can become impassable after precipitation.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding for agate, jasper, and chalcedony near Riverton, Wyoming. Flat terrain, 25 lb daily limit, no permit required.
